coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of highly luminescent graphitized carbon dots and the application in the Hg2+ detection
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yun
Liu, Chun-yan
Zhang, Zhi-ying
coredata.dc:description: Water-soluble graphitized carbon dots (GCDs) were synthesized by the one-step carbonization of ethylene glycol (EG) at 140°C in the presence of concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4). Without any post-passivation, their quantum yield (QY) of photoluminescence (PL) reached 25%. The fluorescence of GCDs was located at about 400nm with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) as small as ∼59nm. The nearly invariable PL position in certain range of excitation wavelength concluded that the good PL of GCDs should be assigned to the bandlike transition similar to general semiconductor. Further investigation showed that they could be used as a label-free fluorescent probe in the selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Photoluminescence
Hg2+ detection
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The influence of metal ions on the fluorescence of GCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of GCDs; (b) HRTEM images of single GCD; (c) the size distribution Histogram of GCDs measured by DLS method.

(a) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the GCDs aqueous solution (350nm excitation). Insert was the photograph of the GCDs aqueous solution under the irradiation of UV lamp. (b) PL spectra of GCDs with the excitation wavelengths from 280 to 500nm. The black line farthest to the left was the excitation spectrum with the emission at 400nm.
The relative fluorescent intensity (I/I 0) of the GCDs aqueous solutions (excitation wavelength 350nm) in the presence of various metal ions (1×10−6 mol/L).

fluorescence spectra of GCDs in the presence of various concentrations of Hg2+ ions (excitation wavelength 350nm) in deionized (A) and tap water (C); the linear relationship between the relative fluorescent intensity of GCDs and the concentration of Hg2+ ions in deionized (B) and tap water (D).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of highly luminescent graphitized carbon dots and the application in the Hg2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectonic Materials, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Zhongguancun, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, No. 29 East Street of Zhongguancun, Haidian District, P.O. Box 2711-47#, Beijing 100190, PR China. Tel.: +86 10 82543573; fax: +86 10 82543573.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble and graphitized carbon dots with photoluminescent quantum yield of 25% have been synthesized by the carbonization of ethylene glycol in the presence of concentrated sulfuric acid, which could be used successfully as a label-free fluorescent probe to selectively and sensitively detect Hg2+ even in real water.

► Graphitized GCDs were prepared by the carbonization of EG in the presence of H2SO4. ► The photoluminescence QY of water-soluble GCDs reached ∼25% without any passivation. ► The GCDs showed good selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Hg2+ ions. ► The PL of GCDs could be quenched by Hg2+ ions mainly through a static quenching mechanism.

Water-soluble graphitized carbon dots (GCDs) were synthesized by the one-step carbonization of ethylene glycol (EG) at 140°C in the presence of concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4). Without any post-passivation, their quantum yield (QY) of photoluminescence (PL) reached 25%. The fluorescence of GCDs was located at about 400nm with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) as small as ∼59nm. The nearly invariable PL position in certain range of excitation wavelength concluded that the good PL of GCDs should be assigned to the bandlike transition similar to general semiconductor. Further investigation showed that they could be used as a label-free fluorescent probe in the selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon and carbon related quantum dots have attracted much attention for the excellent photoluminescence (PL) with chemical stable, photo-stable and non-toxic nature. Until now, many methods have been developed to produce carbon dots (CDs) with high quality, for example pyrolysis of organic molecules [1–4], vigorous chemical oxidation and electro-oxidation of carbon materials [5–7] and laser ablation of carbon aggregations [8,9] and so on. To further improve their PL, surface passivation was generally used. However, surface passivation of CDs with polyethylene glycols or their amide derivatives have to go through very complicate and time-consuming processes, and the quantum yield (QY) of the products was mostly about 10–15% [2,4a,5,8]. Although the surface modification of CDs with oleic amine [4b] or silane [4c] could make their QY reached about 50%, the CDs were only stably dispersed in organic media, limiting their further applications in many cases. In the present work we synthesized water-soluble graphitized carbon dots (GCDs) with graphitized structure by simple carbonization of cheap ethylene glycol in the presence of concentrated H2SO4 at 140°C. Without any surface passivation process the obtained water-soluble GCDs emitted a bright blue light with the QY of ∼25% and a narrow FWHM of ∼59nm.

The other important topic about CDs is to develop suitable applications [10]. Selective and sensitive detection of toxic heavy metal ions in aquatic systems has become a very important issue in the environmental fields, because of their severe menace for our ecology and healthy. Until now, various small organic [11], polygenic [12] and inorganic materials [13–18] have been used to monitor metal ions with spectroscopic method. With a good-chemical stability and excellent PL, fluorescent II–VI, III–V [13–16] nanocrystals and noble metal clusters [17,18], etc. have been developed as fluorescent probes to sensitively detect toxic heavy metal ions such as Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ag+, etc. However, for the existence of virulent heavy metal ions, these materials would result in new environmental and healthy problems. Moreover, to selectively and sensitively respond to certain metal ions, surface functionalization with costly labels such as peptides [19], DNA [20] and thiol-contained compounds [21], etc. through intricate and time-consuming approaches has to be done. All these limited their practical use. Thus, it was very crucial to develop new fluorescent probes with viable process for the qualitative and quantitative detection of toxic heavy metal ions. Our work suggested that the as-prepared GCDs could be successfully used as a label-free fluorescent probe to detect Hg2+ in water. Interestingly, without any surface functionalization, GCDs still showed a good selectivity and sensitivity. Further investigation explained that this good selectivity and sensitivity could be related to the interaction of GCDs and Hg2+.

Analytic grade reagents of concentrated H2SO4, EG, lead chloride (PbCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), barium chloride (BaCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7H2O) and copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. All the reagents were used as received without further purification. Deionized water with the resistivity of 15MΩ was used in experiments in addition to the mention of tap water.

7ml of concentrated H2SO4 was added into 5ml of EG at 140°C and then continuously stirred for 6h. After cooled to room temperature, about 15ml of deionized water was added into the obtained black gel and then stirred for about 30min. Through centrifugation, the supernatant was discarded and then about 30ml deionized water was used to dissolve the precipitation. After stirring for 30min, a yellowish-brown GCDs aqueous solution was obtained by centrifuging the mixture with 12,000rpm for about 15min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of metal ions on the fluorescence of GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the influence of metal ions on the fluorescence of GCDs, standard aqueous solutions of metal ions and GCDs were prepared. The mentioned yellowish-brown GCDs aqueous solution was diluted to one tenth with deionized water to prepare the standard aqueous solution of GCDs. The standard aqueous solutions of metal ions with concentration of 1.00×10−5 mol/L were prepared by dissolving suitable amount of salts in deionized water. To explore the potential of GCDs in the detection of Hg2+ in water, the tap water of Beijing was collected and then used to prepare the standard aqueous solutions of Hg2+ with the concentrations of 5.00×10−3, 1.00×10−3, 1.00×10−5, 1.00×10−6 and 1.00×10−7 mol/L.

The influence of metal ions on the PL of GCDs was done in the following procedures. 0.20ml of the standard GCDs aqueous solution was pipetted into the cuvette, and then appropriate volume of standard metal ions solution was added. After that, the mixture was diluted to the total volume of 10.0ml with deionized water and mixed thoroughly. 30min later, the fluorescence spectrum of the mixture was recorded with the excitation wavelength of 350nm. To explore the potential of GCDs in the detection of Hg2+ in tap water, 0.3ml of the standard GCDs aqueous solution and the tap water of Beijing were used.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded with JEOL JEM-2100 microscope operating at accelerating voltage of 200kV. The IR spectra of the product were measured by the Hitachi Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy with the resolution of 4cm−1 and scan times of 49. IR samples were prepared by dropping the GCDs ethanol solution on the surface of KBr wafer and then dried under illumination of infrared lamp. The Raman spectrum of GCDs was recorded by the Renishaw micro-Raman spectrometer (inVia-Reflex) with the laser of 532nm. The sample was prepared by dropping diluted GCDs aqueous solution on the silver nanoparticle films and then dried at room temperature. Fluorescent spectra of GCDs were monitored with the Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer and the corresponding QY was estimated with quinine sulfate (PL QY 54% in 0.5M sulfuric acid) as a reference [22]. Optical absorption of the GCDs solution at the excitation wavelength was kept smaller than 0.1 to avoid re-absorption. Time-resolved PL spectroscopy was performed with a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) module (Edinburgh Instruments F900) and a temporal resolution of 4ps. A pulse laser (350nm) with an average power of 1mW operating at 40MHz with duration of 70ps was used to excite the GCDs aqueous solution. The particle size of the GCDs in aqueous solution was measured on a Dynapro nanostar (Wyatt, USA) by dynamic light scattering (DLS) method.

Fig. 1a demonstrated that the size of GCDs was in the range of 1–4nm, and the lattice spacing distances calculated for single GCDs were about 0.22, 0.20, 0.23nm (Fig. 1b), in agreement with the graphite (100) plane, meaning the GCDs synthesized here had a structure like graphite. The size distribution of GCDs estimated by DLS method was 0.7–4nm with a relatively narrow size distribution (Fig. 1c), similar to that observed by TEM. Several new bands at 980, 1028, 1190 and 1687cm−1 were observed in the FT-IR spectrum of GCDs, indicating that they contain the functional groups of hydroxyl, epoxy and carboxylic acid (Fig. S1). Two bands at 1360 (D-band) and 1592cm−1 (G-band) were observed in the Raman spectrum of GCDs, confirming the graphitization of GCDs [23,24], which consists with the results of HRTEM. As well known, graphite is the self-assembly of definite polyaromatic network (graphene sheet) linked through π–π interaction [23,24]. Thus, the GCDs prepared here could be reasonably described as polyaromatic networks with limited size linked through π–π interaction and decorated with hydroxyl, epoxy and carboxylic groups.

In the absorption spectrum of GCDs, two sharp bands at 233nm and 293nm and a shoulder at about 350nm were detected (Fig. 2a). Under illumination of UV lamp (∼365nm), GCDs showed bright blue PL (the insert of Fig. 2a), which could be clearly seen by naked eye. Correspondingly, a strong fluorescent band appeared at 401nm. The FWHM of this band was 59nm, obviously narrower than the 100nm previously reported [2,3a,4a,4b,6–9]. Small FWHM of GCDs should be due to the narrower size distribution of the GCDs obtained here; the other possible reason was from the limited processes of radiative recombination of photo-generated carriers.

Single photon counting measurements suggested that there were two fluorescence lifetimes (1.74 and 4.72ns, Table S1) for the emission of GCDs at 400nm, different from the three fluorescence times of poor luminescent CDs [25], implying less radiative processes. It should be noted here, without any post-passivation process, the QY of the water-soluble GCDs reached 25% (quinine sulfate as a reference) under excitation of 340nm [22], which is higher than many passivated CDs [2,3a,4a,4b,6–9] and close to the CDs prepared by the pyrolysis of ethylene–diamine–tetracetic acid salt at 400°C [1]. The fluorescence radiative rate constant (k f
=
ϕ f/τ f) was 6.46×107 s−1 [3b,26,27], larger than that of very good fluorescent dye anthracene (lower than 5×107 s−1) [27], suggesting a stronger electronic transition and molar extinction coefficient.

HRTEM image and Raman spectrum showed that GCDs contain the structure of polyaromatic sp2-hybrid carbon network. FT-IR measurements suggested they had oxygen-containing functional groups (Fig. S1). Simultaneously, in the absorption spectrum of GCDs, there were typical absorptions at 233, 293 and 350nm, respectively. The absorption band at 233nm should be due to the π→π* of CC of polyaromatic sp2-hybrid carbon network and the absorption at 293 and 350nm should be related to the n →π transition of CO or COH bond [28–30]. To further confirm this supposition, we investigated the pH effect on the absorption spectra of GCDs. As shown in Fig.S2, with the pH increase from 0.82 to 5.73, the absorption of GCDs at 293 and 350nm disappeared gradually, while the absorption band at 233nm showed little change. As we known, only carboxyl acid and hydroxyl groups could be influenced by the pH of medium. Therefore, the absorption at 233nm was assigned to the π→π* of CC and the absorption at 293 and 350nm attributed to the n →π transition of oxygen-containing functional groups.

Until now, emission from passivated surface defects [5,6], free zigzag sites [1] and band gap-like transition [3b] had been proposed to explain the PL of CDs. In many works both the fluorescent position and the intensity showed the excitation-dependent nature [1,2,3a,4a,4b,6–9]. By contrast, in the present work, only the PL intensity of GCDs demonstrated the excitation-dependent feature in the wavelength range from 280 to 370nm (Fig. 2b). When an excitation wavelength was higher than 370nm, the fluorescent emission of GCDs red shifted with the excitation to the longer wavelength. Similar phenomenon is often observed in the organic molecules like 2-phenylnaphthalene, in which chromophores combined through CC single bond [31–33]. And as Fig. 2 showed, a sharp band at 352nm in the excitation spectrum of GCDs was very close to the shoulder absorption of GCDs at 350nm. Therefore, it is reasonably to suppose that the fluorescent emission of GCDs at about 400nm should be ascribed to the band gap transition, like the common semiconductor quantum dots [3b].

Fig. S1 suggested that there were various oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GCDs, which could interact with metal ions. On the other hand, the π-electron of polyaromatic networks of GCDs possibly coordinates with transitional metal ions. To explore the selectivity of GCDs in the detection of heavy metal ions, the influence of different metal ions on the fluorescence of GCDs was particularly investigated (Fig. 3 ). It was found that Pb2+, Cd2+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ag+ and Cu2+ ions had little impact on the fluorescence of GCDs whereas Hg2+ ions made it greatly quenched. Furthermore, anions such as SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl− and BO3 3− did not affect the fluorescence of GCDs. Therefore, the GCDs prepared here could be used as a potential label-free fluorescent probe to qualitatively detect Hg2+ ions in an aquatic system. It is worth mentioning that different from the II–IV and III–V nanocrystals labeled by thiols [10a,12b,13,17,25] as well as the CDs passivated [10b,34], the GCDs prepared here showed good selectivity to Hg2+ ions among Hg2+, Ag+ and Cu2+, which may be due to the good coordination and electron-withdrawing ability of Hg2+. Good coordination ability of transitional metal ions improved the interaction between Hg2+ and the π electron of polyaromatic sp2-hybrid carbon network of GCDs. In addition, Hg2+ could also interact with GCDs through coordination with oxygen-containing groups. Moreover, Hg2+ has better electron-withdrawing ability than Zn2+, Cd2+, Ag+ and Cu2+ (the oxidation potential for Hg2+ →Hg+ is +0.920V) [35], and is a better acceptor of electrons [18].

To evaluate the sensitivity of GCDs to Hg2+ ions, aqueous solutions containing different concentrations of Hg2+ ions were added into the reactive solution and recorded the fluorescent spectra of GCDs. Fig. 4A showed that in the deionized water, with the concentration of Hg2+ ions increasing from 0.00 to 1.00×10−5 mol/L, the emission of GCDs decreased gradually. In the range of 0.00–1.00×10−6 mol/L, the relative fluorescent intensity (I 0/I) of GCDs could be described by the Stern–Volmer plot with a perfect linear behavior (R =0.996) and a quenching constant (K sv) of 9.68×105 (mol/L)−1 (Fig. 4B), implying the possibility of GCDs in the quantitative detection of Hg2+ in the deionized water. IUPAC suggested that the detection limit of the spectroscopic analysis should be evaluated by the three times of the standard deviation [36]. Herein, we obtained the detection limit of 3.50×10−8 mol/L, which is obviously lower than that of the typical CdSe/ZnS core shell dots reported by Li et al. [37].

In drinking water, the concentration of alkaline (Na+, K+) and alkaline earth metal ions (Ca2+, Mg2+) could reach hundreds or tens of ppm, obviously higher than those in the deionized water. In order to explore the potential of GCDs to detect Hg2+ in the drinking water, we investigated the influence of Hg2+ on the PL of CDs in the tap water of Beijing. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescent of GCDs in tap water was quenched by the Hg2+. Simultaneously, a linear relationship (R =0.984) existed between the relative fluorescent intensity (I 0/I) of GCDs and the concentration of Hg2+ (linear range 0.00–1.00×10−6 mol/L) with a K sv of 1.97×105 (mol/L)−1 and a detection limit of 6.99×10−8 mol/L (Fig. 4D) [36]. To evaluate the validation of this method, we compared the Hg2+ concentration (in tap water) measured by GCD method with inductively coupled plasma (ICP) optical emission spectrometer. As shown in Table S2, GCD method showed better accuracy than ICP. However, for the environmental monitoring of Hg2+ in the range of 10−8 to 10−9 mol/L [20b], further attention must be paid to the development of more sensitive fluorescent sensor of GCDs.

To clear the quenching mechanism, fluorescence decay of GCDs in the presence of Hg2+ was recorded by the time-resolved PL spectroscopy and the corresponding results were displayed in Table S1. It was found the fluorescence of GCDs decayed with a double exponential function and two fluorescence lifetimes. The addition of Hg2+ gave little influence on the longer fluorescence lifetime of GCDs, whereas it made the shorter fluorescence lifetime decreased to some extent (Table S1). Fig. S3A indicated that Hg2+ made the fluorescent intensity of GCDs obviously reduced. Contrastively, the change of the average fluorescence lifetime of GCDs was rather small, ruling out any dynamic quenching mechanism such as collision quenching. From Fig. S3, it was also found that Hg2+ ions not only made the PL of GCDs obviously quenched but also led to the change of their absorption spectrum, implying the formation of GCDs–Hg2+ complex structure, supporting the static quenching mechanism, too.

A simple carbonization process at moderate temperature has been successfully developed to prepare water-soluble GCDs with very good blue PL and small FWHM. Nearly invariable PL position of GCDs with excitation wavelength from 280 to 370nm implied the PL of GCDs from the band gap transition like common semiconductors. Benefiting from good PL (QY ∼25%), water-soluble and unpassivated nature, the GCDs prepared here exhibited a good selective and sensitive for the detection of virulent Hg2+ in water. In the future, much attention should be paid to the detailed investigation of the formation kinetics of GCDs during carbonization of organic substances and then GCDs with tunable PL would be prepared.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the support of Natural Science Fund of China (NSFC) (21103209 and 21273256).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2012.09.088.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:
